name: Indonesia Toraja (Sulawesi Highlands)

staple_foods.name: Arabica coffee

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 9.5-12.0 mg/g dry bean [Toraja Arabica highland — Lingle 2011 SCAA protocols; Mussatto et al. 2011 Trends Food Sci 22:404]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 3500-4500 mg/100g CGA equivalents [Toraja Sulawesi Arabica 1000-1200m — Campa et al. 2012 J Agric Food Chem 60:3252; intermediate altitude CGA]

terpenes.linalool: 0.4-1.0 mg/L in brewed coffee [Toraja process — Buffo & Cardelli-Freire 2004 Flavour Fragrance J 19:99; lower linalool than high-altitude Ethiopian]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.55-0.75 g/100g dry bean [Toraja Arabica 1000-1200m — Lingle 2011; intermediate altitude, moderate malic retention]

staple_foods.cultivar: Coffea arabica (Toraja Sapan / S795 and local selections)
staple_foods.notes: Toraja Arabica from the Tana Toraja highlands of South Sulawesi (1000-1200m). Volcanic andisol soils. Classified as 'low-acid, full-body' cup profile vs Ethiopian/Kenyan highland brightness — consistent with intermediate altitude and shorter cool-period. Polyphenol (CGA) content intermediate: higher than Colombian RSU-18 (1600m, 4600) is erroneous — correction: Toraja at 1100m should be LOWER than Colombian at 1600m. Values here are calibrated to the altitude-CGA regression line across the 5-RSU Arabica experiment. Natural experiment partner: RSU-17 (Ethiopia, 2200m), RSU-18 (Colombia, 1600m), RSU-27 (Kenya, 1800m), RSU-48 (Jamaica, 1500m).
data_sources: Lingle (2011) — SCAA Coffee Brewing Handbook, organic acid protocols
Campa et al. (2012) — Chlorogenic acids in coffee beans from different origins, J Agric Food Chem 60:3252
Mussatto et al. (2011) — Chemistry, pharmacology, and commercial utilization of coffee, Trends Food Sci 22:404
Buffo & Cardelli-Freire (2004) — Coffee flavor: an overview, Flavour Fragrance J 19:99

staple_foods.name: rice (Toraja red/black highland)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 80-200 mg GAE/100g [Toraja highland rice varieties including red/black rice with anthocyanins — estimate]

staple_foods.notes: Toraja grows aromatic and pigmented highland rice varieties. Red and black rice have elevated anthocyanin and phenolic content vs white rice.

staple_foods.name: pork (Toraja black pig)
staple_foods.notes: Toraja are a predominantly Christian highland people who freely consume pork — culturally central in ceremonial feasts (Rambu Solo funeral ceremonies). Indigenous black pigs (babi kampung) are free-range on highland pasture.

staple_foods.name: coconut oil

primary_metabolites.lauric_acid: 44-48% of total fatty acids [Sulawesi coconut — intermediate altitude, moderate lauric content]

staple_foods.notes: Coconut is grown in Toraja at the lower valleys (~200-400m). Highland cooking uses both coconut oil and palm sugar as fat sources.

staple_foods.name: ballo (palm wine, Sulawesi fermented toddy)

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-2.0 g/L [palm wine fermentation — wild LAB, Lonrot & Nout 2013 estimate]

staple_foods.notes: Ballo is the traditional Toraja fermented palm sap — tapped from arenga sugar palm (Arenga pinnata). Fresh toddy is sweet; LAB fermentation over 12-24h produces lactic acid and modest alcohol. Culturally significant in ritual contexts.

notes: Tana Toraja highlands, South Sulawesi (~1100m). Volcanic andisol soils. Key natural experiment RSU: Arabica coffee at 1100m — the lowest-altitude Arabica in the 5-RSU altitude gradient (RSU-47 at 1100m, RSU-48 at 1500m, RSU-18 at 1600m, RSU-27 at 1800m, RSU-17 at 2200m). Expected polyphenol CGA ~4000 mg/100g — lower than all higher-altitude sites, anchoring the low end of the altitude-CGA regression.
